title: 荣誉附件正文｜LOA full（第3部分）

2) An optimized distributed runtime: Even with the proposed novel algorithm, we still need a runtime to automatically parallelize and distribute the computational process leverage on parallel computing resources to handle a large volume of input data streams. High throughput, low latency processing with good scalability are the key requirements of this runtime. Further, it must provide fault-tolerance guarantee, able to handle out-of-order data (e. g. , missing tuple), and must be able to elastically scale-in and scale-out to wisely utilize hardware resources under varying application demands and input workloads. We propose to build the runtime leverage state-of- the-art stream processing engines, such as Apache Flink with built-in scheduling and fault tolerance mechanisms. 3) An empirical study to confirm the superiority of the proposal: For fair and comprehensive comparison, we also need a standard benchmark to compare the proposed solution against the state-of-the-arts. There are two important aspects to consider in the designing of our benchmark: 1) it must cover a wide range of representative application domains; 2) it must be scalable to suit different scale of input workloads. We systematically examine the profiling results of running the benchmark, with careful considerations on the following dimensions: 1) accuracy: how the accuracy increases/decreases with increasing number of inputs; 2) performance: number of inputs to be processed and the end-to- end prediction latency of each input. Novelty of the Proposal. Social media are primary data sources for sentiment analysis [1]. However, a major problem we are facing is that the data stream generated by social media is continuous, which poses unique challenges making existing solutions mostly no longer adequate. First, existing offline sentiment analyses [2] are not applicable to analyze the public reaction in real-time as streams consist of unbounded, nonstationary data objects that continuously arrive at rapid rates. There are a few attempts [3], [4] at proposing online sentiment learning but unrealistically assumed labelled datasets. For example, Smailovic et al. [4] introduced a stream-based sentiment analysis active learning method based on stock trading. Nicola et al. [3] also proposed an incremental learning algorithm. However, all those works assume there are still hand-labelled datasets. As a result, they are prone to underfit and overfit problems with increasing input tuples. In a similar spirit, Zimmermann [5] proposed to apply streaming clustering to achieve online sentiment classification without relying on labels. However, our preliminary A-2 RTDS S 21 14 01 1 study shows that our proposal is able to achieve much better performance in terms of all three-performance metrics, i. e. , accuracy, throughput and latency. Second, most recent studies of sentiment classification algorithms [6] rely on significant amounts of expert-labelled data, which is scarce, limited by time and privacy constraints. Some unsupervised sentiment classification methods, such as lexicon- based approaches [7] have been proposed.
